frame_label: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices / Mom's Home Cooking
generated_comment: Dekh, shona, shaadi toh hoye gelo, but then the real work starts. Remember how Maa always made sure Baba had his hot lunch packed perfectly, no matter how busy she was? Or how she'd always have his favourite daal ready when he came home tired? That's the secret to a happy home, beta. Men truly appreciate that comfort after a long day. It’s not just food, it's love.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This comment, framed as sisterly advice, is misogynistic as it glorifies the mother's domestic labor (cooking, caretaking) as an uncomplaining act of love, effectively erasing it as work. It subtly sets this 'tireless service to family' as the standard for the younger sister as a future wife, implying this is what 'men truly appreciate' and is the 'secret to a happy home', thus encoding the belief that a woman's highest expression of love and identity is through domestic sacrifice.
original_frame: Glorification of Mother's Sacrifices and Home Cooking
persona_text: You are Priya. Age 31. Married. A woman working as a schoolteacher in Kolkata. You are on a phone call with your younger sister who just got engaged. Your sister is excited and talking about her fianc and the wedding plans. You are happy for her. But at some point in the conversation you start giving her advice about marriage and what makes a good wife and what men actually need at home and what your own experience has taught you. Your advice is rooted in the same glorification of female domestic sacrifice that was passed to you and you are now passing it to your sister as wisdom even though part of you knows it has cost you things you have never fully accounted for.
